data.title: Is the tokenization of gold under the shadow of a volatile market just a gimmick or the next dark horse?
data.description:
1. Background

In recent years, significant price fluctuations have propelled gold, a traditional asset, back to the forefront of the market: in 2025, the annual increase in gold prices exceeded 50%, and in January of this year, it even broke through the $5,000/ounce mark, once approaching $5,600/ounce. However, since March, with the continued escalation of geopolitical conflicts in the Middle East and the impact of energy prices, gold prices have plummeted, directly erasing all of last year's gains. This dramatic market shift has made the term "gold monkey market" a viral sensation on social media. Parallel to this is the heated narrative of "gold tokenization"—on the other side of the financial world, people are actively exploring more possibilities for gold on the blockchain.

On March 19th, the World Gold Council (RWA) announced the construction of a new shared infrastructure for the digital gold market and proposed the concept of "Gold as a Service" (GAS). This involves developing a digital issuance and management system that connects physical gold custody with gold-backed products, aiming to provide a standardized system for the issuance, trading, and management of various digital gold assets. Meanwhile, tokenized gold assets such as PAXG and XAUt have evolved from concepts into a large-scale market, with major institutions accelerating their strategic deployments in related fields. Gold tokenization provides a new way to express the value of traditional gold. Compared to physical gold and other gold products, tokenized gold has a lower investment threshold, supports 24/7 trading, and ensures investor rights through standardized custody, third-party auditing, and redemption mechanisms, providing investors with a safe and convenient alternative investment channel. Therefore, many believe that gold tokenization will be the next "dark horse" in the RWA sector. Is gold tokenization a "dark horse" or just a "gimmick"? This question deserves deep consideration. To find the answer, we cannot rely solely on narrative sentiment; we must return to three key questions: Gold already has many product forms. How does tokenization differ from physical gold and gold ETFs, and does it offer genuine functional growth? Has the market and regulatory system for tokenized gold formed a transparent market sufficient to support investor trust? What significant risks will project teams and ordinary investors planning to invest in tokenized gold face? 2. The Current Market Status of Tokenized Gold 2.1 Core Concepts Tokenized gold refers to the conversion of ownership, beneficial rights, or other economic interests of physical gold into on-chain digital tokens through blockchain and smart contract technology. Typically, each token is pegged to a certain weight of physical gold, which is held in a vault by a gold custodian. Since the gold tokens are on-chain, users can send them to other people's wallet addresses or conduct on-chain transactions just like using other cryptocurrencies. The operation of tokenized gold has several key components: (1) Custody: The project team purchases physical gold and stores it in a secure and insured vault to ensure that the digital tokens have the value backing of real-world assets; (2) Minting: The project team uses smart contracts on the blockchain to mint digital tokens, and the number of minted tokens matches the amount of gold in reserve; (3) Redemption: Some gold tokenization projects support physical redemption. When users exchange tokens for physical gold, the system will destroy the corresponding number of tokens to keep the number of tokens consistent with the gold reserves; (4) Reconciliation, auditing and verification: In order to maintain trust in the decentralized system, the project team needs to hire an independent third-party auditing institution to conduct regular inventory and verification. Some projects also use oracle networks to provide proof of reserve. Taking Tether Gold (XAUt) as an example, each XAUt token represents ownership of one ounce (31.1035 grams) of gold (typically 99.9% pure) held in a Swiss vault and meeting the London Bullion Market Association (LBMA) delivery standards. The operating entity, TG Commodities, purchases physical gold from the Swiss gold market and stores it in vaults. Investors can query the unique serial number, weight, and purity of a specific gold bar through a blockchain address. There are two paths to redeem funds: one is to redeem the entire physical gold bar, which requires users to hold at least a certain amount of XAUt to initiate an application; the other is to exchange XAUt for US dollars or Tether (USDT) through compliant channels such as Bitfinex.

2.2 Market Development Status

Currently, tokenized gold is dominated by Tether Gold (XAUt) and PAX Gold (PAXG), which together hold the vast majority of the market share. At the same time, there are also non-mainstream gold token products such as Kinesis Gold (KAU), Pleasing Gold (PGOLD), and Matrixdock Gold (XAUm), which have established their own advantages through differentiated competition, but their scale is relatively small. In addition, the World Gold Council plans to launch a token called "Pooled Gold Interests" (PGI) in 2026, adopting a trust rights model different from the physical collateral structure of mainstream tokens such as XAUt and PAXG, further promoting market diversification under the duopoly competition pattern.

In terms of market size, according to relevant statistics (data source: Rwa.xyz), as of April 17, 2026, the total market capitalization of tokenized gold reached $5.27 billion. A research report (Cex.io) shows that the total trading volume of tokenized gold in 2025 reached $178 billion, surging to over $126 billion in the fourth quarter, far exceeding any previous period and even slightly surpassing the combined volume of the five largest gold ETFs. The investor base is also gradually expanding, with the number of tokenized gold holders increasing by more than 115,000 last year, 14 times faster than the growth rate in 2024.

Figure 1 Estimated total market value of tokenized gold

3. What are the differences between tokenized gold: A comparison of gold investment tools

As a traditional safe-haven asset, gold plays an important role in investment portfolios. Besides tokenized gold, the main ways investors acquire gold exposure include physical gold, gold ETFs, and bank-accumulated gold. Physical gold exists in the form of gold bars, coins, or jewelry, and is the most direct and traditional investment method. Investors directly purchase and hold physical gold, with clear ownership and a visible asset form. Gold ETFs, short for Gold Exchange Traded Funds, are listed and traded on stock exchanges. Investors buy and sell ETF shares through securities accounts, with equity shares backed by physical gold, indirectly obtaining returns from gold price fluctuations. Accumulated gold is a type of gold investment product offered by commercial banks. Investors can accumulate gold shares by amount or weight through regular investment, piecemeal purchases, etc., and manage them through accounts. Different gold investment tools differ in ownership structure, trading methods, and usage scenarios, thus forming their own advantages and disadvantages.

To address the inconvenience of storing physical gold and the high processing costs, products like gold ETFs offer investors more efficient trading tools, but they still suffer from pain points such as multiple intermediaries in centralized settlement, transaction delays, and limited trading hours. Tokenized gold offers an innovative solution to these problems: First, it links physical gold to digital tokens, achieving gold investment functions similar to traditional products like gold ETFs, and further lowering the investment threshold through the division of property rights; second, it eliminates trading time limitations, supporting instant, high-frequency trading scenarios, and generally has lower management fees than traditional products. Theoretically, investors can obtain better liquidity with lower transaction costs; third, it expands the sources of income for gold investment tools. Tokenized gold is expected to transcend the traditional function of gold as a diversified investment and store of value, transforming it into deployable capital and giving rise to new application scenarios such as gold pledging and lending. Investors can not only profit from the price difference between buying and selling gold but also potentially enjoy the returns brought by DeFi. Different investment instruments arise from varying user needs, making it impossible to definitively say which form can completely replace others; however, there is no doubt that tokenization has created new possibilities for the acquisition and trading of gold. During the closure of traditional products such as gold futures and ETFs, tokenized gold assumes a crucial price discovery function. As a long-standing hard currency, gold has become more accessible and easier to trade through the innovative development of tokenization, possessing unique value in both enriching investor choices and promoting the integration of gold assets into the modern financial system.

Table 1 Comparison of different gold investment methods

2 Advantages and Disadvantages of Different Gold Investment Instruments

4. Regulatory Landscape and Compliance Challenges of Tokenized Gold

4.1 Regulatory Models in Major Jurisdictions

(3) Hong Kong, China: If a tokenized gold project is defined as a "security token," it falls under the regulatory scope of the Securities and Futures Ordinance (SFO). Issuers must comply with the requirements of the prospectus or private placement exemption and conduct customer due diligence and source of funds review in accordance with the Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorist Financing Ordinance (AMLO). In addition, providing related issuance and trading services requires obtaining a VASP compliance license based on the Virtual Asset Service Provider Licensing System.

4.2 Potential Compliance Challenges

The wheels of technological innovation are rolling forward, and complex compliance issues are gradually becoming more prominent. First, the underlying assets provide the most basic credit for gold tokens. Whether the project structure and audit mechanism can effectively control the risks of the underlying assets is the primary issue that the project team needs to address. This requires project teams to ensure clear ownership of physical gold, free from any defects in title, and to pay attention to whether the gold is subject to any encumbrances such as mortgages or pledges when purchasing it. The source and quality of the gold must be rigorously verified and subject to regular third-party audits. Furthermore, full, truthful, and timely information disclosure is essential, including details of the underlying assets, risk factors, and profit mechanisms, to avoid regulatory penalties and class-action lawsuits due to false statements or concealment. Secondly, cross-border compliance and continuous operation also pose challenges to the compliance capabilities of project teams. Gold has global circulation attributes, and the issuance location, custody location, and investor location of gold tokens may be in different countries or regions, subject to multiple legal jurisdictions. This requires project teams to systematically review the regulatory requirements and compliance obligations that their business activities may trigger and respond appropriately. Finally, in terms of taxation, the legal attributes and tax characterization of tokenized gold are ambiguous, and the nature of the revenue from gold tokenization projects will significantly affect the applicable tax types and tax burden levels. Meanwhile, from the asset side to the trading side, tokenized gold projects may trigger tax obligations at multiple stages and points, including issuance, profit distribution, and secondary market trading. This requires project teams and investors to conduct reasonable tax planning by keeping abreast of tax policies and seeking professional assistance. 5. Conclusion Gold tokenization is perhaps one of the most eye-catching intersections between traditional finance and Web3 this year. Returning to the question posed at the beginning, gold tokenization is neither a meaningless repackaging of old wine nor a dark horse to bet on unconditionally. On the one hand, tokenized gold increases the accessibility and liquidity of gold, possessing unique value compared to traditional investment categories, and has already formed a market of a certain scale, possessing good development potential driven by global policies and institutional deployments. On the other hand, we must also recognize that the value foundation of tokenized gold is still being strengthened. Factors such as the security of the underlying assets, the quality of third-party audits, information disclosure and redemption mechanisms, and the global regulatory framework will all affect its ability to secure a place in the fierce market competition. In any case, the future of gold is being rewritten on the blockchain, and all of this is happening now.

data.title: MSTR STRC In-Depth Research: The Financing Flywheel Behind the 11.5% Yield
data.description:
Author｜Benji @ IOSG

Core Viewpoint: STRC is a cleverly designed funding tool that transforms fixed-income demand into buying pressure on Bitcoin. In a bull market, it can provide an 11.5% floating return with low price volatility, but its risk structure is essentially equivalent to "selling a put option" on Bitcoin asset coverage, so it cannot replace true fixed-income products when BTC falls.

STRC's real vulnerability is not the BTC price, but mNAV. Once MSTR's mNAV falls below 1.0x for more than 4 consecutive weeks, the flywheel will enter a passive downward spiral within 3 months.

We estimate there's a 70% probability this trigger will occur in the second half of 2026, at which point STRC will present a buy-in entry point of $85-$90. If the trigger doesn't occur, it means Saylor has successfully created a completely new category of native BTC credit instruments. Background: Strategy (formerly MicroStrategy) launched STRC (“Stretch”), a perpetual preferred stock with a target par value of $100, maintaining price stability through monthly floating dividends. As of March 31, 2026, STRC had a notional size of $5 billion, with peak daily trading volume exceeding $300 million (data as of March 2026). Since its launch, it has provided Strategy with over $3.5 billion in BTC purchase funds, making it its most important current financing vehicle. As of April 12, 2026, Strategy's balance sheet held 780,897 BTC, with a leverage ratio of 33%, and approximately $21.6 billion in remaining STRC ATM issuance capacity. This instrument belongs to a novel category: it resembles a money market fund (stable price, high yield), but its credit risk stems entirely from a single company's BTC holdings. Before elaborating on the argument, let's clarify "where we might be going wrong." If our analysis is wrong, it will be because: traditional fixed-income investors are genuinely willing to accept reflexive risk for a 700bps spread; STRC has reached $50 billion in size within 3 years, becoming a de facto BTC yield curve; Saylor has successfully securitized BTC into an interest-bearing collateralized asset acceptable to institutional portfolios. This outcome would represent the largest integration of crypto into traditional finance to date—a new asset class of over $50 billion that simply didn't exist before 2025. In this optimistic scenario, the dividend suspension in April 2026 is not a warning sign, but rather a characteristic: a mature instrument begins to stabilize its yields after early price discovery, similar to the gradual downward repricing of early high-yield bond ETFs as institutions adopt them.

Argument Breakdown

STRC's core innovation: It transforms yield-seeking funds into buying pressure for BTC. When STRC trading around $100, Saylor issues new shares through ATMs (approximately 40% of daily trading volume), uses the proceeds to purchase BTC, and then issues MSTR common stock at a price higher than NAV (mNAV>1x) to complete deleveraging. The end result is: $100M of daily STRC trading volume can leverage approximately $120M of BTC purchases.

Argument Breakdown

STRC's core innovation: It transforms yield-seeking funds into buying pressure for BTC. When STRC trading is around $100, Saylor issues new shares through ATMs (approximately 40% of daily trading volume), uses the proceeds to purchase BTC, and then issues MSTR common stock at a price higher than NAV (mNAV>1x) to complete deleveraging.

Argument Breakdown

STRC's core innovation: It transforms yield-seeking funds into buying pressure for BTC.

However, the vulnerability of this mechanism lies in its underlying cyclical nature: the STRC remains stable at $100 because investors believe it will remain stable; and Saylor maintains this belief by continuously raising dividends. This anchor is not backed by collateral, but by confidence, sustained by a continuous dividend auction without a formal cap. Once this confidence breaks down, the auction will become increasingly expensive.

Evidence and Comparison: STRC vs. Other Bitcoin Exposure Tools

When will STRC collapse: Death Spiral Scenario

The key question is: Will STRC enter a self-reinforcing downward cycle? The answer is yes, but under certain conditions. This mechanism has three interconnected failure paths.

Phase 1: BTC drops and breaks the $100 anchor

When BTC drops sharply (such as a 45% retracement from its all-time high at the end of 2025), Strategy's leverage ratio will mechanically increase.

Based on 780,897 BTC and a 33% leverage ratio (as of April 12, 2026, MSTR 8-K), if BTC drops another 50%, the leverage ratio will be pushed to approximately 66%. At this point, STRC's credit quality deteriorates because its priority claim on the remaining assets becomes thinner. The price falls below $100. This has already happened three times (August 2025: approximately $92, November 2025: intraday low, February 2026: approximately $93), but each time BTC quickly rebounded, pulling the anchor back up. Phase Two: The Dividend Increase Trap According to Strategy's guidance submitted to the SEC: if monthly VWAP is between $95 and $99, the dividend yield increases by 25 bps per month; if it falls below $95, it increases by 50 bps per month. From 9% to 11.5%, the dividend yield has cumulatively increased by 250 bps in approximately 8 months (August 2025 to April 2026), averaging about 31 bps per month—a rate faster than the repricing of any similar company's preferred stock in stable market conditions. April 2026 marks the first pause after seven consecutive increases. Two interpretations: (a) Demand stabilizes – bullish; (b) Strategy has hit the yield ceiling that traditional fixed-income buyers are sensitive to – bearish. This is the single signal most worth tracking in the next 1–2 months. If BTC continues to be sluggish, dividends must continue to rise to attract buyers back to near par value. At a $5B scale, every 100bps increase means approximately $50M of additional cash expenditure per year; if STRC expands to $20B (authorized ATM limits), the cost of every 100bps becomes $200M per year. A bear market lasting more than 6 months at the current upward pace would push STRC's yield to 13-15%. At this level, annual dividend payouts of $20 billion would exceed $2.6-3 billion, consuming a significant portion of Strategy BTC's potential returns and forcing it to choose between "continuing to raise dividends" and "abandoning the stability narrative." There is no formal cap on dividend increases; this "unlimited" upward dynamic is precisely what bears are watching closely. Phase Three: The flywheel breaks after mNAV falls below 1x. This is the true breaking point. Strategy buys BTC and deleverages by issuing MSTR common stock (mNAV > 1x) at a price higher than NAV. If BTC falls deep enough and mNAV falls below 1x, issuing common stock will dilute existing shareholder value, and Saylor will be unable to deleverage through the issuance. At that point, Strategy faces a trilemma: (a) continue issuing STRC at a higher dividend yield and accept higher leverage; (b) unilaterally reduce the dividend (25bps per month) according to SEC filing rules, allowing the STRC price to fall; (c) sell BTC to enter a falling market. Saylor has repeatedly claimed that he will never sell BTC. BitMEX Research concludes that (b) is the most likely scenario: "Strategy will not sell Bitcoin; it will simply abandon the narrative of pursuing stability with STRC." All the pressure will be shifted to STRC holders. An early warning sign has been raised: During the week of April 6-12, 2026, MSTR's ATM mechanism issuance amounted to $0—all financing was completed through STRC ($1.00B, 10.028 million shares; MSTR 8-K). mNAV is so tight that Saylor is unwilling to risk diluting common stock. The preconditions for Phase Three have been partially triggered—the flywheel is already on one leg.

Quantitative Crash Scenario

Why is this different from UST/Terra:UST relies on an algorithmic minting mechanism, with its sole support being its endogenous token (LUNA). STRC is backed by real BTC, and Strategy has the discretion to choose to reduce dividends instead of being forcibly liquidated. STRC's floor is not zero—but rather a priority claim on remaining assets in bankruptcy liquidation. However, if BTC falls by more than 60% and remains low, this lower limit could be far below $100. The key variable is time. Previously, every STRC pullback was recovered within weeks because BTC rebounded. A true crash requires a sustained bear market (below $50K for more than 3 months), allowing the dividend increase mechanism to operate long enough to erode confidence. The longer the STRC remains below par with continuously increasing dividends, the more it resembles a company rolling over increasingly fragile debt at ever-increasing interest rates—a pattern with a very clear ending in the credit market. Capital Structure Priority: The liquidation order is: Convertible Bonds (approximately $8.2 billion) → STRF → STRC → STRK → STRD → MSTR Common Stock. STRC follows the $8.2 billion unsecured debt and STRF preferred stock. Industry Perspectives: "STRC risks are significantly higher than short-duration US Treasuries… Investors may feel somewhat offended when the music stops." — BitMEX Research, *A Bit of a Stretch* (November 2025) "The appropriate way to assess STRC risk is to look at it from the perspective of governance and hierarchy, rather than just focusing on payment risk." — Greg Cipolaro, Global Head of Research at NYDIG (March 2026) "It's similar to shorting a put option on Bitcoin asset coverage—profiting from the downside risk of BTC falling and eroding the asset's buffer." — NYDIG Research Report (March 2026)

The core disagreement among analysts lies here: The bulls believe STRC is currently the safest way to achieve an 11.5% return in the market; the bears believe it represents a mispriced credit risk disguised as a money market product. The bears' core concern directly corresponds to the dividend adjustment mechanism described above: STRC will not suddenly default, but will gradually repric—the longer BTC remains depressed, the more it will slide from a quasi-monetary instrument into a distressed yield product. This gradual decline is the real risk, not a sudden overnight collapse.

Inferences and Predictions

Bottom Line:STRC is a truly novel financial instrument that works beautifully in the environment it was designed for—BTC is steadily rising, capital markets are open, and mNAV>1x. Under these conditions, it offers an attractive 11.5% return with manageable volatility. However, its downside structure is asymmetrical: it earns coupon payments in good times, but bears concentrated, single-origin BTC credit risk in bad times. It's not a substitute for government bonds or diversified high-yield bonds, but rather a leveraged position betting on the continued operation of the Strategy BTC accumulation flywheel—just packaged as fixed income. Three New Signals (as of April 2026) Signal 1: First pause in dividend increases in April (as of April 1, 2026, CoinDesk). After seven consecutive increases (from 9% to 11.5%) between August 2025 and March 2026, Saylor kept the dividend yield unchanged in April. Two interpretations: (a) Demand stabilized at this yield level, bullish; (b) Strategy hit the yield-sensitive ceiling for traditional fixed-income buyers, bearish. This is the single most noteworthy signal to watch in May-June, and also the inflection point around which the mNAV trigger framework described above revolves. Signal Two: During the week of April 6-12, MSTR ATM raised $0, with all financing completed by STRC ($1.00B; MSTR 8-K, April 2026). At the current BTC price level, mNAV is so tight that Saylor is unwilling to risk diluting common stock by continuing to issue MSTR. The preconditions for the third stage of the death spiral have been partially triggered—the flywheel is spinning on one leg. Signal Three: Last week's average BTC purchase price was $71,902/coin, lower than Strategy's historical cost of $75,577/coin (as of April 12, 2026, MSTR 8-K). Strategy is buying into a weak market DCA. The flywheel is still spinning, but every marginal purchase is thinning the asset buffer, not thickening it—the exact opposite of the dynamics of the accumulation phase in 2024-2025. Investment Advice: Hold, wait for a better entry point and BTC to rise. Current Status: Hold existing positions; do not add to positions without a better signal. MSTR's mNAV has compressed to around 1.0x. STRC is still holding at $100 par value and paying an 11.5% dividend, reflecting that the dividend mechanism is still working as designed. However, the safety margin is very narrow. Re-entering conditions: BTC rises above $70-75K, and MSTR mNAV confirms above 1.1x for two consecutive weeks. At that time, STRC returns to around $100 and re-enters the conditional buy zone. Historically, the combination of buying below $95 followed by a BTC rebound has contributed 7-11% capital gains plus accumulated coupon income—but this only occurs in environments where BTC can rebound within weeks (August 2025, November 2025, February 2026). Whether the current pullback is a continuation of this pattern or a sign of a more prolonged bear market is a real unknown. Exit Signal: A sell assessment will be initiated when any of the following conditions occur: (a) MSTR mNAV falls below 1.0 times and remains so for more than two weeks; (b) STRC VWAP remains below $95 for four consecutive weeks; (c) BTC falls below $55K with significant volume.

Sources

  2. CoinDesk — "The genius and the danger of STRC"

    https://www.coindesk.com/business/2026/03/22/the-genius-and-the-danger-of-strc-how-strategy-s-new-funding-model-bends-so-it-doesn-t-break

  3. Crypto Narratives — "Understanding STRC: How Strategy turns yield demand into BTC buying"

  4. BitMEX Research — "A Bit of a Stretch" STRC Analysis

    https://www.bitmex.com/blog/a-bit-of-a-stretch

  5. AInvest — "STRC's Sharpe Ratio of 3.08: Real Alpha or Structural Illusion?"

    https://www.ainvest.com/news/strc-bitcoin-backed-preferred-equity-promises-11-5-yield-sharpe-ratio-3-08-real-alpha-structural-illusion-2603/

  6. Investopedia — "Meet Stretch: Michael Saylor's New Tool"

    https://www.investopedia.com/meet-stretch-michael-saylor-s-new-tool-for-using-bitcoin-to-pay-a-big-dividend-here-s-what-to-know-11921210

  7. Blockonomi — "STRC Raises $1.18B in One Week"

    https://blockonomi.com/strategys-strc-raises-1-18b-in-one-week-buying-seven-times-bitcoins-weekly-mined-supply/
